_meta.what_firm_does: Notion makes a connected workspace for notes, docs, projects, and knowledge — increasingly with AI agents working alongside people. Its vision centers on humans and agents thinking and building together in one tool.
_meta.jd_wants: The first enablement program manager embedded with Customer Success — translate strategy into training, playbooks, and reinforcement that change CSM behavior in the field, and measure whether it worked.
_meta.status: DRAFT — Jamie to review/edit before submit

Why are you interested in this role?: Great enablement isn't just about producing content—from my experience, it's about translating strategy into programs, playbooks, and training that actually change behavior in the field, then measuring whether they worked. That outcome focus is what pulls me here. At InGenius Prep I own the end-to-end roadmap for 20+ enablement programs and design learning experiences—live sessions, workshops, self-serve resources—for 600+ participants, and I build the playbooks and templates frontline teams actually use. Critically, I set up the measurement and feedback loops that drove a 78% enrollment rate, because I treat behavior change, not content shipped, as the real outcome. Being the first enablement partner for CS is the chance to build that system from the ground up.
Why Notion?: Honestly, because I use Notion daily and have a real opinion about how a tool shapes how people work—that's not flattery, it's lived experience. I'm drawn to where Notion is heading: humans and AI agents working together in one workspace, which means CSMs have to keep getting confident with workflows that are genuinely new. Enabling people through a moment of change is exactly my background. I'd rather build the first CS enablement function at a company whose product I actually believe in and use than run generic training somewhere I'm indifferent to the tool. The fact that the role is new—building the foundation rather than maintaining one—is part of what makes it appealing.
What excites you about this opportunity?: What excites me is that it's a first-of-its-kind role with real ambiguity to shape. There's no existing playbook for enabling CS as it evolves toward deeper AI-workflow adoption, which means the job is to diagnose what CSMs actually need, build the programs, and prove they moved the right behaviors. At NextGen Healthcare I diagnosed performance gaps by analyzing 2,000+ employee experiences, defined the behaviors that mattered most, and led a manager-effectiveness initiative that turned data into measurable improvement—diagnose first, then enable. The chance to bring that diagnose-then-enable approach to a brand-new function, and to help CSMs feel confident and ready rather than just trained, is the part I'd genuinely look forward to.
Tell us about a time you drove adoption: At InGenius Prep, the challenge wasn't producing enablement material—it was getting frontline teams to actually use it and stick with it. I built the playbooks, guides, and templates around what people genuinely needed (not what looked complete), then stood up readiness, adoption, and enrollment feedback loops so I could see what was landing and refine it. That held a 78% program enrollment rate, and the part I'm proudest of is that it came from treating rollout as the start, not the finish. It taught me that great enablement needs both rigorous systems and genuine curiosity about what people actually need—the two reinforce each other. That's exactly the mindset I'd bring to Notion's CSMs.
